feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)

paragraphs.text: Directorate-General for Justice and Consumers European Commission 1049 Bruxelles/Brussel Belgium
24 October 2025
Re: Digital Fairness Act public consultation
Contributors (in alphabetical order):

Emma Alexandre (LLM student, Utrecht University) Niusha Karami (LLM student, Utrecht University) Tomas Liptak (LLM student, Utrecht University) Nadia Otten (LLM student, Utrecht University) Maria Pantoula (LLM student, Utrecht University) Claudia Pezzotta (LLM student, Utrecht University) Iria Roldán Suárez (LLM student, Utrecht University) Merve Savci (LLM student, Utrecht University) Isolde Torres (LLM student, Utrecht University) Sandra Torres (LLM student, Utrecht University) Émie Veyrat-Cotte (LLM student, Utrecht University) Anna Ziková (LLM student, Utrecht University)
To whom it may concern,

This is a response to the solicitation for public comments of the European Commission, relating to the fairness check. This response reflects the discussions of a focus group undertaken as part of the course EU Digital Innovation and Regulation in the context of the Masters Programme Law & Technology in Europe at Utrecht University, where social media influencers have been a focal point. Our response consists of 4 proposals on different subjects of what we think is important to include in the Digital Fairness Act. The first proposal is about the definition of an influencer, the second about harms regarding minors, the third about prohibited practices and the last one about addictive design and unfair personalisation.

"Influencer" is defined as an individual or an identifiable entity in the public eye, whether natural or legal person, or any other persona (including virtual, animal, or AI-based) that engages in activity on at least one social media platform for the promotion of their own person, image, or brand with the primary or ultimate purpose of obtaining an economic advantage. Influencer status shall be determined on the basis of credibility, consistency, longevity, and audience engagement."
The following criteria may be used cumulatively to determine influencer status:
Individual or identifiable entity in the public eye, whether natural or legal person, or any other persona (including virtual, animal, or AI-based);
Activity on at least one social media platform;
Promotion of their own person, image, brand;
Primary or ultimate economic purpose;
Credibility;
Consistency;
Longevity; and
Audience engagement.

Given the growing economic and societal impact of online personalities on consumer behaviour, advertising practices, and market fairness, it is necessary to introduce a clear and future-proof legal definition of the term "influencer" within the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act.

Through personal engagement and participation in social media, we have identified an influencer to be not only an individual but also any identifiable entity that is in the public eye, that engages in an activity on at least one social media platform for the promotion of their own person, image, or brand with the primary or ultimate purpose of obtaining an economic advantage.

This inclusive definition reflects the diverse and technologically evolving online environment, where commercial and promotional activities are not limited to natural persons. Self-promotion aimed at increasing visibility and audience appeal can be carried out by any persona, whether it be natural, corporate, or virtual.

The reference to economic advantage reflects that engagement on social media platforms exceeds mere self-expression, with the ultimate intent to secure financial or commercial opportunities, whether through sponsorships, collaborations, or other economic benefits.

We wanted to stress the importance of the distinction between the terms "influencer" and "content creator." All "influencers" are "content creators", however, not always is a "content creator" an "influencer".

This distinction lies in the intent and effect of their activity. Influencers are characterised by the pursuit of economic gain, while content creators may produce material for artistic, educational, or personal purposes, without such a commercial purpose.
Proposal 2: Harms regarding minors
2.1 Definition of harms regarding minors
Categories of harms:
A. Kidinfluencers
a. exploitation by industry and their families, caregivers (monetization, unfair contracts) b. Harms to child development (physically and mentally health) c. Exposure of personal data
B. Children as users

a. Harms to child development b. Financial harms (avoid one-click purchases + easy access to social media shops) c. Psychological and emotional impacts i. Adoption of inappropriate attitudes ii. Assumption of stereotypical values
2.2 Obligations/Recommendations:
Define kidfluencer
Establishing competencies of child protection authorities
protect from exploitation by industry and their families (monetization)
safeguard child development (physically and mentally health)
influencers who are targeting children, their content must be clearly and prominently disclosed
Platforms shall establish a child-protection compliance mechanism ensuring that algorithms do not amplify harmful influencer content toward minors
Member States shall ensure that regulatory authorities can monitor influencer marketing directed at minors and impose effective, proportionate and dissuasive sanctions.
Educating influencers about children's right
Educating parents and children about their legal responsibilities under product liability laws.
Formal contracts reviewed by independent child age

It is therefore appropriate to include in the list of practices which are in all circumstances unfair provision which, without imposing an out right ban on advertising directed at children, protects them from direct exhortations to purchase (UCPD 18)
Including an advertisement a directive exhortation to children to buy advertised products or pursued their parents or the adults to buy advertised products for them.
Proposal 3: for regulation of prohibited practices within the influencer section of the DFA
UCPD-like system:
Article 6 & 7: misleading actions and omissions
Article 8 & 9: aggressive practices
Article 5: contrary to professional diligence
Annex I
What would this section within the DFA look like?
Article X (based on article 6&7UCPD)

Influencer practices (actions and omissions) that deceive or are likely to deceive the average user*, even if the information is factually correct, in relation to one or more of the following elements, and in either case causes or is likely to cause him to take a transactional decision, decision of political nature, or another decision of similar impact** that he would not have taken otherwise: ...
Average user
→ scholars say that every consumer at one point can be a vulnerable consumer.

→ ECJ Compass Banca: we need to acknowledge that contextual factors such as cognitive biases can influence user behaviour. Users in this online climate do not presumably act ‘reasonably rational’ and observant or circumspect.
** similar impact: you can think of medical decisions like going to a plastic surgeon, a decision to choose particular education or partaking in a course.
Article Y (based on UCPD 8&9)
An influencer practice* shall be regarded as aggressive if, in its factual context, taking account of all its features and circumstances, by harassment, coercion,

including the use of physical force, or undue influence,** it significantly impairs or is likely to significantly impair the average consumer's freedom of choice or conduct with regard to the product and thereby causes him or is likely to cause him to take a transactional decision that he would not have taken otherwise.
In determining whether a commercial practice uses harassment, coercion, including the use of physical force, or undue influence, account shall be taken of:
Maybe influencer practices already fall under the UCPD, but we aim for a lower threshold for the definition of an influencer than a trader to have broader consumer protection.
** With coercion you can think of influencers going live and begging for tokens or gifts. Coercion has a lower threshold within the DFA than within the system of the UCPD, specifically article 9.
Unfair influencer practices under this article shall be prohibited.
An influencer practice is considered unfair, if
a. It is contrary to the requirements of online responsibility and professional diligence *
and
b. It materially distorts or is likely to materially distort the behaviour of the average user, that causes a certain negative impact on their life. **
including but not limited to: honesty, transparency, fairness in presentation or promotion of digital content or products.
** This is a lower threshold

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: Unfair influencer practices

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: Deceptive or misleading practices a. Failure to disclose sponsorships or paid collaborations b. False claims about products or services c. Fabrication of testimonials or results d. Misrepresentation of expertise e. Manipulation through fake scarcity or urgency

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: Harmful or irresponsible content a. Promotion of unsafe products or practices b. Encouragement of illegal behaviour c. Encouragement of disordered behaviour (like glamorizing eating disorders, self-harm, extreme dieting or other harmful lifestyles) d. Dissemination of misinformation or conspiracy theories

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: Exploitative or manipulative conduct a. Targeting vulnerable groups b. Invasion of privacy (not aligning with provisions and obligations of the GDPR) c. Use of fake followers or engagement d. Non-consensual use of other people's content

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: Unethical brand collaboration practices a. Hidden affiliate marketing links b. Promoting or selling of counterfeit products c. Falsifying performance metrics for brand deals

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: Discriminatory or offensive behaviour

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: a. Use of hate speech or discriminatory language b. Harassment or bullying c. Exploitation of children or minors in content

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: Environmental and ethical integrity a. Promotion of environmentally harmful products without disclosure b. Misleading sustainability claims c. Participation in scams or pyramid schemes

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: Digital integrity and transparency a. Manipulating algorithms or engagement artificially b. Deleting critical comments to create a false impression

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: Proposal 4: addictive design and unfair Personalisation

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: Article X

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: Addictive Design

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: a) Platforms should give the option to opt out of addictive design as an easily accessible option and a pop-up for the first time it is shown. If the users opt out the platforms should at least have visible pop-up notifications preventing the user from watching more content, every hour, to indicate to the users how much time has been spent on the application, which disappears only after the user has confirmed to continue using the application.

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: b) Children under 13 cannot have the option to opt-in addictive design.

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: Article X

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: Unfair Personalization

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: a) Consumers shall have the possibility to opt in or out of personalised advertising as an easily accessible option and a pop-up for the first time it is shown.

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: b) Personalized pricing shall be prohibited (To be included under the Article of Prohibited practices).

section_path: Annex I (influencer practices which are in all circumstances considered unfair)
paragraphs.text: c) Due precautions should be taken regarding vulnerable consumers as defined in Article 5(3) of the UCPD, such as a lesser frequency of such personalised content and advertisements, and banners indicating the sensitivity of the content, and designing algorithms in such a way that it does not amplify behaviours linked to vulnerability.
